Both are factors with relatively low volatility ratios (Table 7: $\hat{\sigma}_1/\hat{\sigma}_0 = 2.03$ and $1.93$ under MSI), so the two regimes overlap more strongly in the data and the log-likelihood becomes relatively flat with multiple competing local optima. As a consequence, the MSM and MSI can converge to different parameterisations that fit the data similarly well in this ambiguous region. 


\medskip
\noindent\textbf{Choice of baseline specification.} 

For monthly ifo survey data, the MSI specification provides a more realistic adjustment mechanism. After a regime switch, the factor gradually reverts toward the new regime-specific mean, whereas MSM implies an immediate level shift at the transition. The smooth-adjustment dynamics of the MSI specification is consistent with the idea that manufacturing sentiment adjusts over several months as firms revise their assessments. 

From an estimation perspective, MSI is also numerically more stable, as it only requires filtering over the two marginal regime probabilities $P(s_t \mid \mathcal{I}_{t})$, whereas MSM requires tracking four joint-state probabilities $P(s_{t-1}, s_t \mid \mathcal{I}_{t-1})$. This additional joint-state structure in MSM induces stronger parameter interactions and increases susceptibility to local optima, especially when regimes are weakly separated. Accordingly, we adopt MSI as the baseline specification, and report MSM estimates as a robustness check. The MSM results confirm that the main conclusions are not sensitive to the demeaning convention.





```{r spec-comparison-plots, fig.height=12, out.width="100%", include=FALSE}
# Overlay plots: smoothed P(Turbulent)

overlay_plots <- lapply(1:r, function(j) {
  if (is.null(ms_results_msm[[j]]) || is.null(ms_results[[j]])) return(NULL)

  df <- data.frame(
    date = dates,
    MSM  = ms_results_msm[[j]]$tvtp$prob_smoothed[, 2],
    MSI  = ms_results[[j]]$tvtp$prob_smoothed[, 2]
  ) |>
    pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "Specification", values_to = "P_turbulent")

  ggplot(df, aes(x = date, y = P_turbulent, color = Specification)) +
    geom_rect(data = german_recessions,
              aes(xmin = start, xmax = end, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
              fill = "gray85", alpha = 0.4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, alpha = 0.85) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), labels = percent_format()) +
    scale_color_manual(
      values = c("MSM" = "steelblue", "MSI" = "darkred"),
      breaks = c("MSM", "MSI"),
      labels = c(
        expression(MSM ~ (mu[s[t-1]])),
        expression(MSI ~ (nu[s[t]]))
      )
    ) +
    labs(
      title = paste0("Factor ", j, ": Smoothed P(Turbulent) — MSM vs. MSI"),
      x = NULL, y = "P(Turbulent)", color = NULL
    ) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom", legend.text.align = 0)
})

overlay_plots <- Filter(Negate(is.null), overlay_plots)
if (length(overlay_plots) > 0) wrap_plots(overlay_plots, ncol = 1)
```





\newpage

## C. Out-of-Sample Evaluation of Filtered Crisis Probabilities

The filtered regime probabilities $P(s_t = \text{Turbulent} \mid \mathcal{I}_t)$ are the model's real-time output: at each month $t$, only information available up to $t$ enters the Hamilton filter. However, the full-sample estimates reported in the main text use parameters $\hat{\theta}$ optimised over the entire sample $1, \ldots, T$, so the filtered probabilities are only \textit{pseudo}-real-time. A genuine out-of-sample evaluation requires re-estimating the model on an expanding window $\{1, \ldots, t\}$ and recording the one-step-ahead filtered probability before advancing to $t+1$.

This appendix implements such an evaluation and reports standard probability-forecast scoring metrics.


### Design

We construct a binary recession indicator $d_t \in \{0, 1\}$ from the manufacturing recession dates used throughout the main text:

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
Episode & Start & End \\
\midrule
Dot-com / early-2000s & 2001-01 & 2001-12 \\
Global Financial Crisis & 2008-01 & 2009-06 \\
Euro-area Sovereign Crisis & 2012-01 & 2013-03 \\
COVID-19 & 2020-01 & 2020-06 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

For the purpose of this evaluation, they serve as a rough benchmark rather than a precise turning-point chronology. 

\medskip
\textbf{Expanding-window protocol.}
Let $t_0$ denote the start of the evaluation period. We set $t_0 =$ 2007-01 (month 192), which provides an initial training window of approximately 15 years containing the 2001 recession. At each evaluation month $t = t_0, t_0 + 1, \ldots, T_{\text{eval}}$:

\begin{enumerate}
\item Re-estimate the TVTP model on $\{1, \ldots, t\}$ using the same optimizer, penalty constraints, and leading-indicator pipeline as in the main text.
\item Run the Hamilton filter on $\{1, \ldots, t\}$ and record the \textbf{final filtered probability} $\hat{p}_t = P(s_t = 1 \mid \mathcal{I}_t; \hat{\theta}_{1:t})$.
\item As a benchmark, also run a \textbf{fixed-TP model} (no leading indicator) and record its filtered probability.
\end{enumerate}

The evaluation ends at $T_{\text{eval}} =$ 2019-12 (just before the COVID episode) to avoid the post-2020 structural break issue. A separate evaluation including 2020 is shown for completeness but interpreted with caution.

\medskip
\textbf{Scoring rules.}
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Brier Score}: $\text{BS} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{eval}} - t_0 + 1} \sum_{t=t_0}^{T_{\text{eval}}} (\hat{p}_t - d_t)^2$. Lower is better.
\item \textbf{AUC}: Area Under the ROC Curve, computed from the ranked filtered probabilities against $d_t$. AUC $= 0.5$ is random; AUC $> 0.7$ is considered useful discrimination.
\end{itemize}


```{r oos-load-results, include=FALSE}
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Load pre-computed expanding-window results from server
# (See 05_MS_DFM_OOS.Rmd for the estimation code)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

oos_path    <- "output/ms_models/ms_oos_evaluation.rds"
has_oos     <- file.exists(oos_path)

if (has_oos) {
  oos_results <- readRDS(oos_path)
  cat("Loaded OOS evaluation results from:", oos_path, "\n")
} else {
  cat("OOS results not found — run 05_MS_DFM_OOS.Rmd on server first.\n")
}
```


### Results

```{r oos-scoring-table, results='asis', eval=has_oos}
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Table: Scoring metrics — TVTP vs Fixed TP vs Base Rate
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Construct recession dummy over evaluation dates
eval_dates <- oos_results$eval_dates
d_t <- as.numeric(eval_dates >= as.Date("2008-01-01") & eval_dates <= as.Date("2009-06-01") |
                  eval_dates >= as.Date("2012-01-01") & eval_dates <= as.Date("2013-03-01"))

# If evaluation extends to 2020
if (max(eval_dates) >= as.Date("2020-01-01")) {
  d_t_full <- d_t | (eval_dates >= as.Date("2020-01-01") & eval_dates <= as.Date("2020-06-01"))
} else {
  d_t_full <- d_t
}

# Pre-2020 evaluation window
pre2020 <- eval_dates < as.Date("2020-01-01")
d_pre   <- d_t[pre2020]
base_rate_pre <- mean(d_pre)

score_df <- data.frame(Factor = integer(), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

for (j in 1:r) {
  if (is.null(oos_results$filtered_tvtp[[j]])) next

  p_tvtp  <- oos_results$filtered_tvtp[[j]][pre2020]
  p_fixed <- oos_results$filtered_fixed[[j]][pre2020]

  # Brier Score
  bs_tvtp  <- mean((p_tvtp - d_pre)^2,  na.rm = TRUE)
  bs_fixed <- mean((p_fixed - d_pre)^2, na.rm = TRUE)
  bs_ref   <- mean((base_rate_pre - d_pre)^2)

  # AUC (simple trapezoidal, avoids pROC dependency)
  auc_fn <- function(p, d) {
    n1 <- sum(d == 1); n0 <- sum(d == 0)
    if (n1 == 0 || n0 == 0) return(NA)
    ranks <- rank(p)
    (sum(ranks[d == 1]) - n1 * (n1 + 1) / 2) / (n1 * n0)
  }
  auc_tvtp  <- auc_fn(p_tvtp,  d_pre)
  auc_fixed <- auc_fn(p_fixed, d_pre)

  score_df <- rbind(score_df, data.frame(
    Factor    = j,
    BS_TVTP   = round(bs_tvtp, 4),
    BS_Fixed  = round(bs_fixed, 4),
    BS_Ref    = round(bs_ref, 4),
    AUC_TVTP  = round(auc_tvtp, 3),
    AUC_Fixed = round(auc_fixed, 3),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  ))
}

score_df |>
  kable(
    format    = "latex",
    booktabs  = TRUE,
    col.names = c("Factor",
                  "BS (TVTP)", "BS (Fixed)", "BS (Ref.)",
                  "AUC (TVTP)", "AUC (Fixed)"),
    caption   = paste0(
      "Out-of-Sample Probability Forecast Evaluation (2007-01 to 2019-12). ",
      "BS = Brier Score (lower is better); ",
      "AUC = Area Under ROC Curve."
    ),
    escape    = FALSE,
    align     = "ccccccccc"
  ) |>
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Brier Score" = 3, "AUC" = 2)) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("H", "scale_down"), full_width = FALSE)
```


```{r}
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Table: Scoring metrics — TVTP vs Fixed TP vs Base Rate (Full Sample)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# 1. Construct the comprehensive recession dummy for all dates
eval_dates <- oos_results$eval_dates

# Define recession periods: GFC, Euro Debt, and COVID+
d_full <- as.numeric(
  (eval_dates >= as.Date("2008-01-01") & eval_dates <= as.Date("2009-06-01")) | # GFC
  (eval_dates >= as.Date("2012-01-01") & eval_dates <= as.Date("2013-03-01")) | # Euro Debt
  (eval_dates >= as.Date("2020-01-01") & eval_dates <= as.Date("2020-06-01"))   # COVID-19
)

# 2. Calculate the Base Rate over the full evaluation period
# This represents the "naive" forecast probability (unconditional mean)
base_rate_full <- mean(d_full)

# Initialize the results data frame
score_df <- data.frame(Factor = integer(), stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# 3. Calculate metrics for each factor using the full time frame
for (j in 1:r) {
  # Skip if the results for this factor are missing
  if (is.null(oos_results$filtered_tvtp[[j]])) next

  # Use the full vector of filtered probabilities instead of the pre-2020 subset
  p_tvtp  <- oos_results$filtered_tvtp[[j]]
  p_fixed <- oos_results$filtered_fixed[[j]]

  # Calculate Brier Scores (MSE of probability forecasts)
  # Lower values indicate better calibration and sharpness
  bs_tvtp  <- mean((p_tvtp - d_full)^2,  na.rm = TRUE)
  bs_fixed <- mean((p_fixed - d_full)^2, na.rm = TRUE)
  bs_ref   <- mean((base_rate_full - d_full)^2)

  # Calculate AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve)
  # Measures the ability to discriminate between recession and expansion states
  auc_fn <- function(p, d) {
    n1 <- sum(d == 1); n0 <- sum(d == 0)
    if (n1 == 0 || n0 == 0) return(NA)
    ranks <- rank(p)
    (sum(ranks[d == 1]) - n1 * (n1 + 1) / 2) / (n1 * n0)
  }
  
  auc_tvtp  <- auc_fn(p_tvtp,  d_full)
  auc_fixed <- auc_fn(p_fixed, d_full)

  # Store results
  score_df <- rbind(score_df, data.frame(
    Factor    = j,
    BS_TVTP   = round(bs_tvtp, 4),
    BS_Fixed  = round(bs_fixed, 4),
    BS_Ref    = round(bs_ref, 4),
    AUC_TVTP  = round(auc_tvtp, 3),
    AUC_Fixed = round(auc_fixed, 3),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  ))
}

# 4. Generate the LaTeX table via kable
# Updated the caption to reflect the full sample range
max_date_label <- format(max(eval_dates), "%Y-%m")
min_date_label <- format(min(eval_dates), "%Y-%m")

score_df |>
  kable(
    format    = "latex",
    booktabs  = TRUE,
    col.names = c("Factor",
                  "BS (TVTP)", "BS (Fixed)", "BS (Ref.)",
                  "AUC (TVTP)", "AUC (Fixed)"),
    caption   = paste0(
      "Out-of-Sample Probability Forecast Evaluation (", min_date_label, " to ", max_date_label, "). ",
      "BS = Brier Score (lower is better); ",
      "AUC = Area Under ROC Curve. Ref. is the unconditional base rate."
    ),
    escape    = FALSE,
    align     = "c"
  ) |>
  add_header_above(c(" " = 1, "Brier Score" = 3, "AUC" = 2)) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("H", "scale_down"), full_width = FALSE)
```









```{r oos-turning-points, results='asis', eval=has_oos, include=FALSE}
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Table: Turning-point lead analysis
# For each crisis episode in the evaluation window, report the first month
# where filtered P(Turbulent) > 0.5, and the lead (months) relative to
# the recession start date.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────


episodes <- data.frame(
  name  = c("GFC", "Euro Slowdown", "COVID-19"),
  
  # GFC: CEPR Euro Area Dating Committee (2008 Q1 - 2009 Q2)
  # Euro: Bundesbank Dec 2012 report — mild contraction Q4 2012 - Q1 2013
  # COVID: Bundesbank 2020 — deep recession Q1-Q2 2020
  start = as.Date(c("2008-01-01", "2012-10-01", "2020-02-01")),
  end   = as.Date(c("2009-06-01", "2013-03-01", "2020-06-01"))
)

tp_rows <- list()

for (j in 1:r) {
  if (is.null(oos_results$filtered_tvtp[[j]])) next

  p_tvtp_all  <- oos_results$filtered_tvtp[[j]]
  p_fixed_all <- oos_results$filtered_fixed[[j]]

  for (ep in 1:nrow(episodes)) {
    ep_start <- episodes$start[ep]
    # Only include episodes within the evaluation window
    if (ep_start < min(eval_dates)) next

    # Look for first P > 0.5 within 12 months before episode start
    window_start <- ep_start %m-% months(12)
    window_end   <- ep_start %m+% months(3)  # allow 3 months into episode
    in_window    <- eval_dates >= window_start & eval_dates <= window_end

    if (sum(in_window) == 0) next

    # TVTP: first crossing > 0.5
    p_sub <- p_tvtp_all[in_window]
    d_sub <- eval_dates[in_window]
    first_cross_tvtp <- if (any(p_sub > 0.5, na.rm = TRUE)) {
      d_sub[which(p_sub > 0.5)[1]]
    } else {
      NA
    }

    # Fixed: first crossing > 0.5
    p_sub_f <- p_fixed_all[in_window]
    first_cross_fixed <- if (any(p_sub_f > 0.5, na.rm = TRUE)) {
      d_sub[which(p_sub_f > 0.5)[1]]
    } else {
      NA
    }

    # Lead in months (positive = signal before recession start)
    lead_tvtp <- if (!is.na(first_cross_tvtp)) {
      as.numeric(difftime(ep_start, first_cross_tvtp, units = "days")) / 30.44
    } else {
      NA
    }
    lead_fixed <- if (!is.na(first_cross_fixed)) {
      as.numeric(difftime(ep_start, first_cross_fixed, units = "days")) / 30.44
    } else {
      NA
    }

    tp_rows[[length(tp_rows) + 1]] <- data.frame(
      Factor   = j,
      Episode  = episodes$name[ep],
      Signal_TVTP  = if (!is.na(first_cross_tvtp)) format(first_cross_tvtp, "%Y-%m") else "—",
      Lead_TVTP    = if (!is.na(lead_tvtp)) sprintf("%+.0f", round(lead_tvtp)) else "—",
      Signal_Fixed = if (!is.na(first_cross_fixed)) format(first_cross_fixed, "%Y-%m") else "—",
      Lead_Fixed   = if (!is.na(lead_fixed)) sprintf("%+.0f", round(lead_fixed)) else "—",
      stringsAsFactors = FALSE
    )
  }
}

tp_df <- bind_rows(tp_rows)

tp_df |>
  kable(
    format    = "latex",
    booktabs  = TRUE,
    col.names = c("Factor", "Episode",
                  "First Signal", "Lead (mo.)",
                  "First Signal", "Lead (mo.)"),
    caption   = paste0(
      "Turning-Point Lead Analysis. ",
      "``First Signal'' = first month where filtered $P(\\text{Turbulent}) > 0.5$. ",
      "``Lead'' = months before recession start (positive = early warning, negative = late). ",
      "``---'' = no signal within 12-month pre-crisis window."
    ),
    escape    = FALSE,
    align     = "clcccc"
  ) |>
  add_header_above(c(" " = 2, "TVTP" = 2, "Fixed TP" = 2)) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("H"), full_width = FALSE) |>
  collapse_rows(columns = 1, valign = "middle", latex_hline = "major")
```


```{r oos-probability-plots, fig.height=14, out.width="100%", eval=has_oos}
# Figure: Out-of-sample filtered P(Turbulent) over time

german_recessions <- data.frame(
  start = as.Date(c("2001-01-01", "2008-01-01", "2012-01-01", "2020-01-01")),
  end   = as.Date(c("2001-12-01", "2009-06-01", "2013-03-01", "2020-06-01"))
)

oos_plots <- lapply(1:r, function(j) {
  if (is.null(oos_results$filtered_tvtp[[j]])) return(NULL)

  df <- data.frame(
    date     = eval_dates,
    TVTP     = oos_results$filtered_tvtp[[j]],
    Fixed_TP = oos_results$filtered_fixed[[j]]
  ) |>
    pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "Model", values_to = "P_turbulent")

  ggplot(df, aes(x = date, y = P_turbulent, color = Model)) +
    geom_rect(data = german_recessions,
              aes(xmin = start, xmax = end, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf),
              fill = "gray85", alpha = 0.4, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50", linewidth = 0.3) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.6, alpha = 0.8) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), labels = percent_format()) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("TVTP" = "steelblue", "Fixed_TP" = "darkred")) +
    labs(
      title = paste0("Factor ", j, ": Out-of-Sample Filtered P(Turbulent)"),
      subtitle = "Expanding-window re-estimation, parameters estimated on {1, ..., t} only",
      x = NULL, y = "P(Turbulent)", color = NULL
    ) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")
})

oos_plots <- Filter(Negate(is.null), oos_plots)
if (length(oos_plots) > 0) wrap_plots(oos_plots, ncol = 1)
```







Several patterns emerge from the out-of-sample exercise.

First, the Brier Skill Scores indicate whether the regime-switching model adds value beyond simply predicting the base rate of manufacturing recessions. A positive BSS means the model's real-time crisis probabilities are more informative than a constant forecast. Since the unconditional recession frequency in the evaluation window is itself a strong benchmark (manufacturing recessions are not rare in this sample), even modest positive skill scores represent meaningful discrimination.

Second, the comparison between TVTP and fixed-TP models isolates the contribution of the leading indicator. If TVTP achieves higher AUC or better turning-point lead, this confirms that the indicator provides genuine early-warning information beyond what the regime-switching structure alone can deliver. If the two models perform similarly, this is consistent with the main-text finding that BIC favors fixed TP: the indicator signal is present but too weak to improve real-time forecasting at the margin.

Third, the turning-point lead analysis provides the most directly interpretable metric for policy applications. A lead of 2--3 months before a recession date is substantial for a monthly indicator; leads of 0 or negative values indicate that the model recognises crises only contemporaneously or after onset. Note that the 0.5 threshold is conventional and that a lower threshold (e.g., 0.3) would yield earlier but noisier signals.

\medskip
\noindent\textit{Caveats.}
The evaluation window contains only 2--3 crisis episodes depending on the end date, so the scoring metrics should be interpreted as descriptive rather than as formal tests of forecast accuracy. The AUC in particular can be volatile with so few positive observations. Additionally, the factors and leading indicators are estimated on the full sample in Sections 02--04, introducing a degree of look-ahead bias that a fully recursive design would avoid but at prohibitive computational cost. The expanding-window re-estimation of the MS-AR parameters in this appendix mitigates the most direct source of in-sample contamination (regime-switching parameter estimates), but does not eliminate it entirely.
